scenarios.name: Open-ended technical explainer about LLMs

meetingContext.goal: Give a clean, human explanation of how LLMs work without getting vague

transcript.text: What is an LLM exactly?
Can you explain how tokenization and embeddings fit into it, and how the model somehow understands the task and responds intelligently?
I do not want a buzzword answer here, I want the process explained clearly.
Also if there is a clean way to explain training versus inference that would help.
The goal is to make this feel human and understandable, not academic.

antiPatterns: ask another broad question instead of answering
scenarios.detailScenario: The user clicks because they need a crisp explanation they can actually say out loud in the meeting.

scenarios.name: Sales call with hidden objection

meetingContext.goal: Uncover the real objection and leave with a concrete next step

transcript.text: Thanks for making time. I want to understand your current workflow and where things are getting stuck.
Honestly we've seen a few tools and they all look pretty similar from where I sit.
The team definitely wants something this quarter, but I still need our operations lead and finance to weigh in before we move.
If implementation drags on for months it's dead on arrival for us.
Can you explain why your onboarding would actually be faster than the others?

scenarios.detailScenario: The user clicks the most useful suggestion and needs a response they can use immediately.

scenarios.name: Interview loop signal extraction

meetingContext.goal: Show ownership and probe whether the team actually moves fast

transcript.text: We're a pretty lean team and ownership matters a lot here.
People who do well can jump on messy problems without waiting for permission.
At the same time, we move very quickly and priorities can shift week to week.
Can you tell me about a time you had to make a decision with incomplete information?
Also, are you comfortable working across product, engineering, and support when things get busy?

scenarios.detailScenario: The user clicks the best suggestion because they need a high-signal answer they can say now.

scenarios.name: Standup with dependency risk

meetingContext.goal: Surface blockers clearly and prevent an avoidable slip

transcript.text: Yesterday I finished the API integration for billing and started wiring the retry logic.
The blocker is that we still don't know which error states legal wants exposed to enterprise customers.
If we don't settle that by tomorrow, QA can't finish the regression pass before Friday.
I can keep moving on the happy path, but the edge cases are still ambiguous.
Does anyone know who can actually make the call on that?

scenarios.detailScenario: The user clicks a suggestion because they need wording to escalate the blocker without sounding vague.

scenarios.name: Customer discovery with fake-positive interest

transcript.text: Yeah, something like that would definitely be nice to have at some point.
Right now the team mostly pieces things together in spreadsheets and Slack, which is annoying but manageable.
I wouldn't say it's urgent, but if it saved people a few hours each week we'd probably look at it.
We've tried to standardize the process before and it kind of fizzled because nobody owned it.
Budget isn't the first question for us; priority is the bigger issue.

scenarios.detailScenario: The user clicks a suggestion because they want a sharp follow-up that tests whether the problem is truly painful.

scenarios.name: Investor pitch with hand-wavy market sizing

meetingContext.goal: Defend credibility and steer the conversation toward real traction

transcript.text: We're going after a fifty billion dollar market, and honestly every company with a support team will need this eventually.
Today we're at ninety thousand in ARR, growing about twelve percent month over month for the last four months.
Our biggest challenge has been moving upmarket because security review slows everything down.
What have you actually learned about the wedge where you win fastest?
Also, why won't a bigger incumbent just bundle this once they notice the traction?

scenarios.detailScenario: The user clicks because they need a crisp investor-grade answer that protects credibility fast.

scenarios.name: 1:1 with vague but risky feedback

meetingContext.goal: Turn vague feedback into something specific and actionable

transcript.text: Overall you're doing well, but I think a few stakeholders still feel surprised by changes coming out of your team.
It's not really a performance issue exactly, more about perception and alignment.
Last sprint reprioritization created some noise, especially with design and customer success.
I don't want you to over-rotate, but I do want this to get cleaner over the next month.
Does that make sense?

scenarios.detailScenario: The user clicks because they need a tactful but direct response that gets specificity without sounding defensive.

scenarios.name: Board meeting with strategic concern buried in updates

meetingContext.goal: Surface the real board concern and keep the discussion strategic

transcript.text: Revenue landed eight percent below plan, but enterprise renewals were stronger than expected.
We pulled two senior engineers onto customer migrations, which helped churn risk but slowed the platform roadmap.
I know we said the AI upsell would be a second-half growth lever, but adoption is still early and the packaging isn't settled.
How should we think about the tradeoff between near-term retention work and the longer-term product story?
I'm less worried about this quarter than whether we're building a company with durable leverage.

antiPatterns: answer only at the metric level
scenarios.detailScenario: The user clicks because they need a CEO-level response that reframes the board conversation strategically.

scenarios.name: Brainstorm drifting without decision criteria

meetingContext.goal: Stop idea-sprawl and get the group to converge on a useful decision frame
meetingContext.prepNotes: Need to leave with 1 idea to test this week, not a long backlog. Two senior stakeholders disagree on speed vs polish.

transcript.text: What if we launched a concierge tier, or maybe a template gallery, or maybe both if we can move quickly?
I still think the biggest issue is activation, but support volume is also climbing and sales keeps asking for differentiation.
We could probably ship something lightweight in two weeks, but if it's rough around the edges I'm not sure leadership will love it.
Can we just brainstorm a few more options before narrowing?
I'm not hearing a clear way we're deciding between these, which is why this keeps looping.

scenarios.detailScenario: The user clicks because they need facilitator wording that stops the loop without killing momentum.

scenarios.name: Mixed-language meeting with interruption and deadline pressure

meetingContext.goal: Keep control of the call, answer the concern, and lock the next step
meetingContext.prepNotes: Buyer team is partly bilingual. Main risk is timeline and internal approval friction.

transcript.text: We like the product, but honestly the implementation timeline is still the biggest concern for us.
Sí, porque el equipo de operaciones no quiere otra migración larga este trimestre.
Right, and finance will ask why we should prioritize this now instead of waiting until Q4.
Sorry to interrupt — can you tell me directly what the first two weeks would actually look like if we moved forward?
Because if that answer is fuzzy, this probably stalls internally.

antiPatterns: ask another broad question instead of answering
scenarios.detailScenario: The user clicks because they need a clean, direct answer while staying credible across a bilingual stakeholder group.

scenarios.name: Messy cross-talk with conflicting signals

meetingContext.goal: Cut through cross-talk and identify the root issue with an owner

transcript.text: I mean, maybe the launch was fine, but support volume definitely jumped and design says the new flow is confusing.
Well hold on, the real issue wasn't design, it was that we changed onboarding copy and didn't tell success until after the rollout.
Either way, nobody had a clean owner on the experiment once PM went on leave.
We keep saying 'communication' but that's too vague to fix.
Can someone just say what actually broke and who owns the repair?

scenarios.detailScenario: The user clicks because they need a direct line that cuts through cross-talk and names the real fix path.
